title: Ability
whenToUse: To express physical or mental skills and capabilities
formula: Subject + can/could/be able to + base verb
examples: She could swim when she was five.
They will be able to join us tomorrow.
commonMistakes: Using 'to' after can (e.g., I can to swim)
Using 'can' for past ability instead of 'could'

title: Advice
whenToUse: To give suggestions, recommendations, or opinions about what is right
formula: Subject + should/ought to/had better + base verb
examples: You should see a doctor about that cough.
We ought to leave early to avoid traffic.
You had better finish your report tonight.
commonMistakes: Using 'should to' (should is followed directly by base verb)
Forgetting that 'had better' is a strong warning, not just a casual suggestion

title: Obligation
whenToUse: To express requirements, rules, or strong necessity
examples: I must remember to call my mother.
Students have to wear a uniform.
You mustn't smoke in the building.
commonMistakes: Using 'mustn't' when you mean 'don't have to' (mustn't = prohibited; don't have to = optional)
Using 'must' for past obligations instead of 'had to'

title: Probability
whenToUse: To express how certain we are that something is true
examples: It's cloudy; it might rain later.
He hasn't eaten all day; he must be hungry.
That can't be true; I saw him in London yesterday.
commonMistakes: Using 'must' for negative certainty (use 'can't' instead of 'mustn't' for logical deduction)
Confusing 'may' (possibility) with 'can' (general ability) in formal contexts
